subject: Physics
unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power
questions.question: What is the weight of a \(5\text{kg}\) mass on Earth? (Use \(g = 9.8\text{m/s}^2\))
questions.explanation: Weight \(W = mg = 5\text{kg} \times 9.8\text{m/s}^2 = 49\text{N}\). Option A divides mass by g, option D doubles g.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power
source_trace.content_anchor: weight of a body of mass 10 kg = 98 N

explanations_tiered.concept_summary: On Earth, g ≈ 9.8 m/s², so 5 kg weighs 49 N.
explanations_tiered.beginner: A 5 kg bag of sugar weighs about 49 newtons — that's like holding 5 kg under gravity.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Weight is gravitational force: \(\vec{W} = m\vec{g}\). Magnitude = 49 N directed downward.
worked_solution: Step 2: Substitute m=5 kg, g=9.8 m/s².
misconceptions: Confusing mass (5 kg) with weight (49 N).
Using g = 10 gives 50 N, but precise g=9.8 gives 49 N.

glossary_entry.definition: Gravitational force on mass m: W = mg.

publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Remind students weight is force, unit newton.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power
questions.question: Which direction does kinetic friction act relative to an object's motion?

options.A: In the same direction as motion
options.C: Opposite to the direction of motion

questions.explanation: Kinetic friction opposes the sliding motion, so it acts opposite to the direction of motion. Option A would speed the object up, which friction does not do.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power

explanations_tiered.definition: Kinetic friction always acts opposite to the direction of sliding motion.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Friction resists relative motion, so it points opposite to velocity.
explanations_tiered.beginner: When you slide a box, friction pulls backward, opposite to the slide direction.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Kinetic friction vector is opposite to velocity vector: \(\vec{f}_k \uparrow\downarrow \vec{v}\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Friction force = \(-\mu_k N \hat{v}\), where \(\hat{v}\) is unit vector in direction of motion.
reasoning_steps: If object moves right, friction acts left.
misconceptions: Some think friction always acts downward — direction depends on motion.

flashcard.front: Direction of kinetic friction relative to motion?

publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use diagram: sliding right, friction left.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Kinetic friction opposes motion.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power
questions.question: How much vertical force is required to lift a load vertically through \(2\text{m}\) if \(800\text{J}\) of work is done?
questions.explanation: \(W = Fd \Rightarrow F = W/d = 800\text{J} / 2\text{m} = 400\text{N}\). Option A multiplies work and distance.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power
source_trace.content_anchor: force required to lift a load to a height of 3 m if work done is 600 J → 200 N

explanations_tiered.definition: Force = work / displacement when force is parallel to displacement.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Rearrange \(W = Fd\) to solve for force.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If 800 J of work lifts something 2 m, the force needed is 400 N.
misconceptions: Multiplying work × distance (800×2=1600 N) is a common error.

publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Calculate force from work and displacement

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power
questions.question: A \(0.5\text{kg}\) ball is thrown at \(12\text{m/s}\). What is its kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: \(E_k = \frac{1}{2} \times 0.5\text{kg} \times (12\text{m/s})^2 = 0.25 \times 144 = 36\text{J}\). Option A uses only m×v (no square, no half).

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power
source_trace.content_anchor: 200 g ball at 20 m/s → Ek = 40 J

explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Compute speed squared first, then multiply by mass and ½.
explanations_tiered.beginner: A 0.5 kg ball at 12 m/s has 36 J of motion energy.
misconceptions: Using mv (0.5×12=6 J) forgets square and half (option B).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power
questions.question: A machine does \(2400\text{J}\) of work in \(40\text{s}\). What is its power?
questions.explanation: \(P = W/t = 2400\text{J} / 40\text{s} = 60\text{W}\). Option A divides time by work.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power

explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Divide work by time to get power in watts.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If 2400 J of work is done in 40 seconds, power is 60 watts.
worked_solution: Step 2: Substitute W=2400 J, t=40 s.
misconceptions: Dividing time by work (40/2400 ≈ 0.0167 W) is incorrect.

publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Calculate power from work and time

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power
questions.question: Is force a vector or scalar quantity?
questions.hint: Force has both magnitude and direction.
questions.explanation: Force is a vector because it has both magnitude and direction. Scalars (like mass) have magnitude only. Option A is incorrect.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power
source_trace.content_anchor: tell whether a force is a vector or scalar quantity

explanations_tiered.definition: A vector has both magnitude and direction; a scalar has only magnitude.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Force has a direction (e.g., 10 N to the right), so it is a vector.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Force is a vector because you push or pull in a direction.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Force is represented by an arrow showing magnitude and direction → vector.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Force satisfies vector addition (commutative, parallelogram law) and transforms as a vector under rotation.
misconceptions: Some think force is scalar because it is often given as a number — but direction matters.

publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Force is a vector because it has direction.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power
questions.question: When a car suddenly stops, passengers lurch forward. Which law explains this?
questions.explanation: Newton's first law (inertia): passengers continue moving forward at constant velocity while car stops, because no net force acts on them horizontally. Option B relates force to acceleration, not inertia.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power
source_trace.content_anchor: If the car comes to a sudden stop, your body tends to keep moving forward

explanations_tiered.definition: Newton's first law: an object in motion stays in motion unless acted on by a net force.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Passengers continue forward due to inertia when the car stops.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Your body wants to keep moving because of inertia when the car stops suddenly.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Newton's first law: no horizontal force on passengers → they maintain forward velocity while car decelerates.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Inertial reference frame: car decelerates, passengers' velocity constant relative to ground → appear to lurch forward.
reasoning_steps: Recall inertia: objects resist change in motion.
misconceptions: Some think a force pushes them forward — no, it's absence of force (inertia).
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Sudden stop → lurch forward = inertia (Newton I).

glossary_entry.definition: Tendency to resist changes in motion.

flashcard.front: Car stops suddenly, passengers lurch forward. Which law?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Car stops suddenly, passengers lurch forward. Which law?
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Apply Newton's first law to real-world situations
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Newton's first law (inertia) explains continued forward motion.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power
questions.question: When a pendulum swings upward, what happens to its kinetic and potential energy?
questions.explanation: As pendulum rises, speed decreases (kinetic energy decreases) while height increases (gravitational potential energy increases). Total mechanical energy remains constant (ignoring friction).

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power
source_trace.content_anchor: energy conversion between kinetic and potential

explanations_tiered.definition: Energy conservation: total mechanical energy = kinetic + potential.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: During upward swing, kinetic converts to potential; speed drops, height rises.
explanations_tiered.beginner: At the top of a swing, you're highest and slowest — potential high, kinetic low.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Gaining height increases \(E_p = mgh\); losing speed decreases \(E_k = \frac{1}{2}mv^2\).
misconceptions: Thinking both increase or both decrease violates energy conservation.

glossary_entry.definition: Energy transforms but total remains constant.

flashcard.front: Pendulum swings upward. Ek and Ep change?

publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Changing from one form to another.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer D. Kinetic decreases, potential increases (energy conservation).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power
questions.question: A 100 W light bulb and a 2000 W kettle are both on for 10 seconds. Which statement is correct?

options.D: Energy usage depends on voltage only

questions.explanation: Energy \(E = P \times t\). Kettle: \(2000\text{W} \times 10\text{s} = 20000\text{J}\). Bulb: \(100\text{W} \times 10\text{s} = 1000\text{J}\). Kettle uses more energy in same time.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power

explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Higher power device uses more energy in same time.
explanations_tiered.beginner: A 2000 W kettle uses much more energy than a 100 W bulb in the same time.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Energy transfer rate (power) × time = total energy transferred.
misconceptions: Thinking equal time means equal energy — false; power matters.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Higher power → more energy in same time.

flashcard.front: 100 W bulb vs 2000 W kettle, 10 s. Which more energy?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: 100 W vs 2000 W for 10 s — which more energy?
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Relate to electricity bills: kWh = power × hours.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Compare energy usage from power ratings
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Kettle: 2000×10=20000 J; bulb: 100×10=1000 J → kettle uses more.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power
questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a contact force?
questions.explanation: A hand pushing a box requires direct touch → contact force. Gravity (A), magnetism (B), and electrostatic (D) are non-contact forces.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power

explanations_tiered.definition: Contact forces arise from physical touch between objects.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Pushing, pulling, friction, normal force are contact forces.
explanations_tiered.beginner: A hand pushing a box is a contact force because they touch.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Contact force requires direct physical contact; gravity and magnetism do not.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Contact forces are mediated by electromagnetic interactions at the atomic scale when surfaces touch.
reasoning_steps: Hand pushes box → touch → contact force.
Other options act at a distance.
misconceptions: Gravity is non-contact; some think it requires touch.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Contact forces: push, pull, friction (touch required).

publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Hand pushing a box requires physical contact → contact force.
